title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
text:
Overview


Every change starts in a branch
These branches merge to main







Copyright © 2023 IBM Corporation

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
text:
Everything starts with a branch:




  gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      branch feature-001 order: 5
      commit

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
section: When on a branch, developers commit changes in isolation:
text:
When on a branch, developers commit changes in isolation:




%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans', 'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      branch feature-001 order: 5
      commit
      commit






Every branch can be built and tested. ## When ready, the feature branch can be reviewed and approved to merge into main:

%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans',
           'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      branch feature-001 order: 5
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-001

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
section: Multiple features can be developed concurrently:
text:
Multiple features can be developed concurrently:
%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans',
           'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      branch feature-001 order: 5
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      branch feature-002 order: 6
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-001
      checkout feature-002
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-002

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
text:
or serially:
%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans',
           'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      branch feature-001 order: 5
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      branch feature-002 order: 6
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-001
      branch feature-003 order: 6
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-003
      checkout feature-002
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-002

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
section: And then a release candidate can be created:
text:
And then a release candidate can be created:
%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans',
           'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      commit
      branch release-2.1.0 order: 2
      commit id:"rel-2.1.0"
      checkout main
      commit
(note: commits to main here are, of course, merges of approved branches)

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
section: Fixes are handled as branches from main, which are tested, approved, merged to main and cherry-picked to add to the release branch:
text:
Fixes are handled as branches from main, which are tested, approved, merged to main and cherry-picked to add to the release branch:
%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans',
           'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      commit
      branch release-2.1.0 order: 2
      commit id:"rel-2.1.0"
      checkout main
      branch hot-fix-2.1.0.1 order: 5
      commit id:"hf-2.1.0.1"
      checkout main
      merge hot-fix-2.1.0.1
      checkout release-2.1.0
      cherry-pick id:"hf-2.1.0.1"
      checkout main
      commit

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
section: Features can run over release boundaries:
text:
Features can run over release boundaries:
%%{init: { 'fontFamily': 'IBM Plex Sans',
           'logLevel': 'debug', 'theme': 'base', 'gitGraph': {'showBranches': true, 'showCommitLabel':true,'mainBranchOrder': 4}} }%%
      gitGraph
      commit
      commit
      branch feature-001 order: 5
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      branch feature-002 order: 6
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-001
      branch release-2.1.0 order: 2
      commit id:"rel-2.1.0"
      checkout feature-002
      commit
      commit
      checkout main
      merge feature-002

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
text:
Agenda

Aims and Assumptions
Choosing a branching model and worflow
Starting simple
Scaling up
Integration branches
Delivering changes via a release
Hot-fix for production
Using an Epic branch

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
text:
Aims and Assumptions
Some aims and assumptions that guide our recommendations include:

The workflow and branching scheme should both scale up and scale down.


Small teams with simple and infrequent changes will be able to easily understand, adopt, and have a good experience.
Large, busy teams with many concurrent activities will be able to plan, track, and execute with maximum agility using the same fundamental principles.

title: The workflow for  git-based z/OS DevOps
text:
Aims and Assumptions - planning

Planning and design activities as well as code development aim to align to a regular release cadence.
There is no magic answer to managing large numbers of “in-flight” changes, so planning assumptions should aim as much as possible to complete changes quickly, ideally within one release cycle.

DevOps/Agile practices typically encourage that, where possible, development teams should strive to break down larger changes into sets of smaller, incremental deliverables that can each be completed within an iteration. This reduces the number of “in-flight” changes, and allows the team to deliver value (end-to-end functionality) more quickly while still building towards a larger development goal.

We know it is sometimes unavoidable for work to take longer than one release cycle and we accommodate that as a variant of the base workflow.
